plainMeaning: Worshipping and praising the Jina, praising one's teacher, showing love to fellow Jains, keeping one's dealings honest, and joining chariot-processions and pilgrimages are duties of a devout lay Jain.
recitation: jiṇapūā jiṇathuṇaṇaṁ, guruthua sāhammiāṇa vacchallaṁ; vavahārassa ya suddhī, rahajattā titthajattā ya

wordByWord.gloss: worship of the Jina; the ritual veneration of the Tīrthaṅkara
wordByWord.etymology: jiṇa (Skt. jina, 'victor') + pūā (Skt. pūjā, 'worship'); Prakrit -jā > -ā with the long ū kept verbatim per the book
wordByWord.notes: First of the devotional acts; the physical/aṣṭaprakārī honouring of the image, paired below with verbal praise

wordByWord.etymology: jiṇa + thuṇaṇaṁ (Skt. stavana/stunana, from √stu, 'to praise'); accusative-neuter -aṁ
wordByWord.notes: Verbal devotion (stuti) complementing the act of pūā — worship in deed, then in word

wordByWord.etymology: guru (Skt. guru) + thua (Skt. stuta/stava, √stu); shortened sandhi form thua
wordByWord.notes: Devotion now turns from the Jina to the living teacher; sets up the closing 'suguru-upadeśa' refrain of v.5

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. sādharmika ('one of the same dharma'); Prakrit genitive plural -āṇa
wordByWord.notes: Joins with the next token to form the glossary compound sādharmika-vātsalya

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. vātsalya, from vatsa ('calf'); the cow's instinctive love for her calf — hence selfless affection
wordByWord.notes: vacchallaṁ + sāhammiāṇa = sādharmika-vātsalya; the social-duty centre of the verse

wordByWord.gloss: of conduct / of one's worldly dealings
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. vyavahāra ('transaction, dealing'), vi + ava + √hṛ; Prakrit genitive sing. -assa
wordByWord.notes: Genitive governing suddhī below: 'purity of dealings'
Links vyavahāra-śuddhi into the running list

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. śuddhi, from √śudh ('to be pure'); long final -ī verbatim
wordByWord.notes: With vavahārassa = vyavahāra-śuddhi, honesty/integrity in commerce and conduct

wordByWord.gloss: the chariot-procession of the Jina image (ratha-yātrā)
wordByWord.etymology: raha (Skt. ratha, 'chariot') + jattā (Skt. yātrā, 'going, procession'); y- > j- is regular in Prakrit
wordByWord.notes: A public, communal act of devotion

wordByWord.etymology: tittha (Skt. tīrtha, 'ford, holy site') + jattā (Skt. yātrā); doubled -tth- per the book
wordByWord.notes: Paired sound-alike with rahajattā, closing the verse on outward pilgrimage
Final connective leaving the catalogue open into v.4

literalTranslation: Worship of the Jina, praise of the Jina, praise of the guru, affection toward co-religionists; and purity of (one's) dealings, the chariot-procession, and pilgrimage.
idiomaticTranslation: To worship the Jina and sing his praise, to honour one's teacher, to cherish one's fellow Jains with heartfelt love, to keep one's every dealing clean and honest, to join the sacred chariot-procession and to make pilgrimage to the holy tīrthas — these too are the devotee's duties.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This gāthā gathers six duties and arranges them in a deliberate movement outward from the self. It opens with the two faces of devotion to the Jina: pūā (worship in deed — the offered flower, lamp and water) and thuṇaṇaṁ (worship in word — the sung stava). Reverence then steps from the liberated, wordless Jina to the living guru (guru-thua), because for the householder the teacher is the audible voice of the silent Tīrthaṅkara. From the vertical axis of devotion the verse pivots to the horizontal axis of community: sāhammiāṇa vacchallaṁ, the calf-tender love (vātsalya) owed to one who shares one's dharma — not mere tolerance but active, affectionate support. Strikingly, the very next item is vavahāra-suddhī, purity in one's worldly transactions. Placing commercial honesty immediately beside temple-worship is the verse's quiet argument: for a Jain merchant-householder, an unblemished ledger is itself an act of religion, the natural extension of ahiṁsā and satya into the marketplace. The closing pair, rahajattā and titthajattā, both built on -jattā (yātrā, 'going'), turn faith into movement — the chariot that brings the Jina out to the streets, and the pilgrim who goes out to the Jina's holy places. The doubled ya leaves the list grammatically unfinished, handing the catalogue on to the inner virtues of the following verse.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse encodes the classical śrāvaka principle that devotion (bhakti) and ethics (cāritra) are inseparable: vyavahāra-śuddhi makes ahiṁsā and satya concrete in livelihood, while sādharmika-vātsalya operationalises the four-fold Saṅgha's mutual obligation. Worship is graded — image-pūjā, verbal stuti, guru-reverence — reflecting the dravya-bhāva (substance/feeling) logic of Jain ritual. Ratha- and tīrtha-yātrā are forms of prabhāvanā in embryo, raising the tradition's esteem and the doer's puṇya.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: A reciter is reminded that daily worship is incomplete without honest business and warmth toward fellow practitioners: keep the ledger clean, help a struggling co-religionist, attend the chariot-procession, and undertake at least one pilgrimage as a lived, not merely intended, devotion.

crossReferences.text: Ratha-yātrā and tīrtha-yātrā as lay devotional duties
crossReferences.source: Śvetāmbara śrāvakācāra literature (Yogaśāstra, Hemacandra, on the householder's observances)

sources.title: Manaha Jinanam Sajjay — Jain Sutras and Stotras
sources.consultedFor: verse text and the enumeration of jina-pūjā, guru-stuti, sādharmika-vātsalya, vyavahāra-śuddhi, ratha- and tīrtha-yātrā as śrāvaka duties

sources.title: Can you give the whole Manaha Jinanam Anam sutra in English
sources.consultedFor: English rendering of this gāthā's six duties

sources.title: Mannaha Jinan Aanam Swadhyay — Book Summary (JainGPT)
sources.consultedFor: later printed commentary tradition and confirmation of the verse's place among the śrāvaka-kṛtya

notes: Tokenisation gives 11 tokens at 100% coverage; sāhammiāṇa + vacchallaṁ together form the single glossary compound sādharmika-vātsalya. Orthography is kept verbatim from the book: the long ū of જિણપૂઆ, the doubled conjuncts of વચ્છલ્લં / તિત્થજત્તા, and the unspaced comma in 'સુદ્ધી,રહજત્તા' are reproduced as printed, not normalised. The two ય particles are read as the connective ca (Pkt. ya). The trailing ya leaves the catalogue open into v.4, consistent with the enumerative structure noted in the preface. The web-engine glosses that read સાહમ્મિઆણ as 'forbearance' and રહજત્તા as 'seclusion/renunciation' are erroneous folk-readings and are rejected in favour of the attested sādharmika and ratha-yātrā per the tradition knowledge base and Prakrit lexica.
